title: Baker-Bowler Theory for Lagrangian Grassmannians
speaker: Donggyu Kim
affiliation: Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology
abstract:
Baker and Bowler (2019) showed that the Grassmannian can be defined over a tract, a field-like structure generalizing both partial fields and hyperfields.
This notion unifies theories for matroids over partial fields, valuated matroids, and oriented matroids.
We extend Baker-Bowler theory to the Lagrangian Grassmannian which is the set of maximal isotropic subspaces in a 2n-dimensional symplectic vector space.
By Boege et al. (2019), the Lagrangian Grassmannian is parameterized into the projective space of dimension 2n−2(4+(n2)−1 and its image is cut out by certain quadrics.
We simplify a list of quadrics so that these are apparently induced by the Laplace expansions only concerning principal and almost-principal minors of a symmetric matrix.
From the idea that the strong basis exchange axiom of matroids captures the combinatorial essence of the Grassmann-Pl\"{u}cker relations, we define matroid-like objects, called antisymmetric matroids, from the quadrics for the Lagrangian Grassmannian.
We also provide its cryptomorphic definition in terms of circuits capturing the orthogonality and maximality of vectors in a Lagrangian subspace.
We define antisymmetric matroids over tracts in two equivalent ways, which generalize both Baker-Bowler theory and the parameterization of the Lagrangian Grassmannian.
It provides a new perspective on the Lagrangian Grassmannian over hyperfields such as the tropical hyperfield and the sign hyperfield.
Our proof involves a homotopy theorem for graphs associated with antisymmetric matroids, generalizing Maurer's homotopy theorem for matroids.
We also prove that if a point in the projective space satisfies the 3
-/4-term quadratic relations for the Lagrangian Grassmannian and its supports form the bases of an antisymmetric matroid, then it satisfies all quadratic relations, which is motivated by the earlier work of Tutte (1958) for matroids and the Grassmannian.

proof_stages.label: Motivation from matroids with coefficients and the symmetric-matrix obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The talk situates the problem in Baker--Bowler matroids over tracts and delta/even-delta matroids, then shows that nonsingular principal minors of symmetric matrices do not satisfy the delta-matroid exchange axiom.

proof_stages.label: Almost-principal minors and restricted Grassmann--Plücker relations
proof_stages.summary: To repair the failure of principal minors alone, the speaker adds almost-principal minors, encodes row and column choices in a doubled ground set, and derives one restricted Grassmann--Plücker-type relation from Laplace expansion.

proof_stages.label: Basis axioms for antisymmetric matroids
proof_stages.summary: Transversals and almost transversals are defined, and an antisymmetric matroid is axiomatized by transpose-closure plus an exchange condition modeled on the restricted Plücker relations, with comparisons to delta matroids and even delta matroids.

proof_stages.label: Low-degree tests and circuit cryptomorphism in the unvalued case
proof_stages.summary: The restricted relations are reduced to three- and four-term tests under the antisymmetric support condition, and circuits are introduced as minimal non-bases satisfying a skew-pair restriction with axioms analogous to matroid circuit/cocircuit painting.

proof_stages.label: Coefficient framework and antisymmetric F-matroids
proof_stages.summary: After reviewing tracts, nullsets, and F-matroids, the speaker defines antisymmetric F-matroids as projective Plücker vectors satisfying the symmetry condition and the restricted Grassmann--Plücker relations over an arbitrary tract.

proof_stages.label: Antisymmetric F-circuit sets and cryptomorphism theorem
proof_stages.summary: Circuit vectors over a tract are defined from minimal supports with the Lagrangian orthogonality condition, and the main cryptomorphism theorem gives a bijection between antisymmetric F-matroids and antisymmetric F-circuit sets, recovering the Lagrangian Grassmannian cut-out in the field case.

proof_stages.label: Homotopy theorem for the general-tract proof
proof_stages.summary: To prove well-definedness and the circuit--basis equivalence over general tracts, the speaker introduces the transversal base graph and proves a homotopy theorem saying its first homology is generated by bounded-weight cycles, generalizing Maurer's matroid base-graph theorem.

proof_stages.label: Relations to sign, tropical, symplectic, and polytopal theories
proof_stages.summary: The closing discussion relates antisymmetric matroids over special tracts to oriented matroids, valuated matroids, symplectic tropical linear spaces, isotropic tropical linear spaces, and possible type-C/polytopal formulations.

examples.name: Ordinary matroids as even delta matroids
examples.note: The speaker points out that matroids give even delta matroids when all bases have the same cardinality, making the symmetric-difference exchange axiom concrete without driving the new construction.
examples.engagement_reason: He identifies ordinary matroids as even delta matroids with all bases of the same cardinality, but does not work through a concrete matroid example.

examples.engagement_reason: He gives a concrete symmetric principal-minor counterexample, choosing bases such as the empty set and {1,2,3} and checking that no exchange element f exists.

examples.name: Different tracts with the same pasture
examples.note: In response to a question about tracts versus pastures, the speaker cites the regular pasture and a product involving the Krasner tract to show that truncating to pasture data can identify distinct tracts.
examples.engagement_reason: He explains pasteurizing a tract by discarding longer sums and compares specific distinct tracts that become the same pasture.
